cuisine: Cantonese Chinese / Hong Kong / Singapore Chinese
region_of_origin: Hong Kong / Guangdong, China — widely adopted in Singapore
food_subgroup: Chilled agar or gelatin-set mango pudding with evaporated milk and fresh mango

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Cantonese mango pudding. Closest parent is a fruit-flavoured gelatin or agar-based chilled dessert. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chilled mango pudding — agar or gelatin, evaporated milk, sugar, fresh mango puree
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0FGF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for a fruit-flavoured chilled gelatin/agar dessert; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Cantonese mango pudding specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific dessert preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard individual mango pudding serving is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a single mould as served at dim sum restaurants or sold pre-packaged in Singapore supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Mango Pudding — Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Foodo; values estimated from component ingredients
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact HPB Foodo entry for Cantonese agar-based mango pudding was available. Values were estimated from component ingredients (fresh mango, evaporated milk, agar-agar, sugar) using HPB Singapore Foodo as the primary reference, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for mango (USDA #09176) and evaporated milk (USDA #01086). All macros and micronutrients are consistent with the component-based estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Mango (Mangifera indica) GI reference; analogous estimate applied to mango pudding
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cantonese agar-based mango pudding. GI of 58 (medium) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fresh mango (~55–60), which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor. The agar/gelatin matrix and evaporated milk fat moderately slow glucose absorption. GL per 150 g serving = (58 × 30.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.9. Classified as medium GI, moderate GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (42 mg/100 g); moderate potassium (118 mg/100 g) and calcium (72 mg/100 g) from evaporated milk
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving delivers only 63 mg sodium — well below the 600 mg per-meal threshold recommended for hypertensive individuals — and the calcium from evaporated milk may provide a modest blood-pressure benefit; mango pudding is one of the lowest-sodium Chinese desserts available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~58) and moderate glycemic load (GL 17.9 per 150 g serving) driven by mango natural sugars and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one small mould (approximately 100–120 g) to keep the glycemic load below 12, and should account for the 18.5 g/100 g sugar content when planning daily carbohydrate intake; the milk fat in evaporated milk provides a modest moderating effect on postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (2.2 g/100 g), negligible trans fat (0.0 g/100 g), low sodium (42 mg/100 g), and meaningful vitamin C (18 mg/100 g) and beta-carotene/vitamin A (42 mcg RAE/100 g) from mango
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mango pudding has a favourable cardiovascular profile for a dessert — low saturated fat, zero trans fat, and low sodium — and the mango-derived vitamin C and beta-carotene provide antioxidant support; a standard 150 g serving contributes only 3.15 g saturated fat, well within the 10–15 g daily limit for cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Pudding (芒果布丁 / 芒果布甸) is a classic Cantonese chilled dessert that originated in Hong Kong and Guangdong province and has become a staple of Singapore's Chinese dessert culture. It is made by blending fresh ripe mango puree with evaporated milk and sugar, then setting the mixture with agar-agar (vegetarian) or gelatin in individual porcelain or plastic moulds. Once chilled and set, the pudding is turned out onto a plate and typically finished with a drizzle of evaporated milk and a slice of fresh mango. The colour is a vivid golden-orange, the texture is silky-smooth and barely set — softer than a Western gelatin dessert — and the flavour is purely, intensely mango with a gentle dairy creaminess. It is served at dim sum restaurants as a dessert course, at Chinese dessert stalls (糖水铺), and is widely available pre-packaged in Singapore supermarkets. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving provides approximately 177 kcal, 3.75 g protein, 5.25 g fat, and 30.75 g carbohydrate. It is a meaningful source of vitamin C (27 mg per serving) and beta-carotene/vitamin A (63 mcg RAE per serving) from the mango, and provides moderate calcium (108 mg per serving) from evaporated milk. Sodium is very low at 63 mg per serving. The GI is approximately 58 (medium) with a glycemic load of 17.9 per serving, making it a moderate-GL dessert. It is suitable for vegetarians and is halal and gluten-free when made with agar-agar.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients using HPB Singapore Foodo as the primary reference, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for mango and evaporated milk. No single exact HPB or USDA entry for Cantonese agar-based mango pudding exists; the estimation is well-grounded but not derived from a direct laboratory analysis of this specific preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database mango entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this agar-based mango pudding preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per HPB component data for evaporated milk and mango puree. All other per_100g values are numeric and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo component estimates cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database mango entry (~55–60); no direct GI measurement for Cantonese mango pudding is available in the approved GI database. FoodEx2 code A0FGF is used as the closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific dessert. FoodOn URI is null — no specific FoodOn concept for mango pudding exists; closest parent is a fruit-flavoured agar/gelatin chilled dessert.
